concerns.text: Aggregating daily sentiment to quarterly averages before testing for stationarity or cointegration risks severe information loss. Quarterly aggregation of high-frequency sentiment data may smooth out the very 'drift' or lead-lag signals the project aims to detect, rendering the subsequent Granger causality tests underpowered or misleading. The plan must justify this frequency mismatch or propose a higher-frequency alignment (e.g., monthly) if quarterly economic data is the only constraint.

The plan proposes Granger causality tests on quarterly data with an assumed 20+ year history (~80 data points). Granger causality tests require sufficient degrees of freedom; with 3-4 variables and optimal lags (likely 2-4 quarters), the effective sample size for the regression will be critically low (<60 observations). This creates a high risk of Type II errors (failing to detect true causality) and unstable coefficient estimates, undermining the 'causal validity' of the conclusions.

The Moving Block Bootstrap (MBB) block length is set to '4 weeks' (1 month) while the data is aggregated to 'Quarterly' frequency. This is a unit mismatch. For quarterly data, the block length must be in quarters (e.g., 1-4 quarters) to preserve the autocorrelation structure. A 1-month block on quarterly data is effectively a block size of 0.25, which fails to capture the temporal dependence required for valid bootstrap inference.

The sensitivity analysis masks '[deferred] to [deferred]' of data. While placeholders are allowed, the methodology lacks a defined range for this masking. Masking too little (e.g., 1%) may not stress-test the model, while masking too much (e.g., 50%) destroys the time-series structure. The plan must specify a scientifically grounded range (e.g., 5% to 20%) to ensure the sensitivity analysis actually validates robustness rather than just noise.
